title: Freehand: a page that makes sense without eyes
why.empty: Empty editor. Do not start from the code. Draw the page on paper — rectangles. Then name each one. The code is just writing the drawing down.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed, or closed in the wrong order.
hint2.not-closed: Wrappers nest, they do not overlap. <main><article></main></article> is wrong — the inner one closes first.
why.no-h1: There is no page heading, or it is empty. One <h1>, with words in it. How else will a person know where they have landed?
hint2.no-h1: The title of your site. The name of the thing you are building. It sits at the top, in the <header>.
why.no-header: There is no top part. The logo and the site title sit somewhere — but the browser does not know that is the top part unless you tell it.
hint2.no-header: There are two things at the top: the site name and the menu. One wrapper holds both.
hint4.no-header: <header> opens at the start of the body and closes after the menu.
why.no-nav: There is no navigation. A list of links is not a menu until you call it a menu.
hint2.no-nav: Even a single strip with three words is navigation. Say so with <nav>.
why.nav-empty: The navigation is there, but it leads nowhere. A menu without links is a list of words. At least two are needed.
hint2.nav-empty: Each menu item is an <a href="...">. The files do not exist yet and that is fine — what matters is that the link exists.
hint4.nav-empty: Check where you close the </nav> too. Close it before the list and the links end up outside the navigation.
why.no-main: There is no main content. If everything on the page is equally important, then nothing is. Say which part is the point.
hint2.no-main: What does a person come to your page for? That goes in <main>. The menu and the bottom part do not.
why.many-main: There is more than one <main>. It is exactly one per page. Two main contents means zero.
hint2.many-main: All the standalone things live inside ONE <main>. Not one each.
why.no-article: There are not two standalone things inside the main content. The task asks for at least two — articles, listings, recipes. Something that would still make sense on its own, lifted out of the page.
hint2.no-article: What repeats in your topic? A recipe. A listing. A review. Each of those is an <article>.
hint3.no-article: The test: cut it out and paste it on a stranger's site. Does it make sense alone? A recipe — yes. An About us section — no, that is a <section>.
hint4.no-article: The two articles sit side by side, inside <main>.
why.empty-article: There is a standalone thing, but there is nothing inside it. An empty shell is not a listing — it is an empty shell.
hint2.empty-article: Every article wants content: a heading, text, an image. Whatever fits your topic.
why.no-h2: The standalone things have no headings. A blind visitor jumps from heading to heading — that is how they skip what they do not need. With no headings there is nowhere to jump.
hint2.no-h2: <h1> is the heading of the PAGE. Each listing inside has its own heading — <h2>.
hint4.no-h2: One <h2> inside each article, at the top.
why.no-footer: There is no bottom part. It is not decoration — it is where the content ends and the formalities begin.
hint2.no-footer: Copyright. Contacts. The fine print. The stuff that is on every page of the site.
why.empty-footer: The bottom part is an empty box. Put something in it — a copyright, a year, a contact.
why.too-many-div: More than one <div>. The rule was: one, and only if there truly is no tag for what you are building.
hint2.too-many-div: Search with Ctrl+F for the word div. For each one ask: is there really no tag for this thing?
hint4.too-many-div: Top part, navigation, main, standalone thing, side note, bottom part, heading, list, paragraph — they all have tags. What is left is the logo.
blocks.2.text: Your topic, again. But this time you have one extra condition, and it is the whole test:
blocks.3.text: Write the page so that someone could understand it without seeing it.
blocks.4.text: It must have: a top part with a logo or site title, navigation with real links, main content, at least two standalone things inside it (articles, listings, recipes — whatever fits your topic), each with its own heading, and a bottom part.
blocks.5.text: Each of those has its own tag. You know them all.
blocks.7.text: One <div> is allowed. Just one, and only if there truly is no tag for what you are building. If you use two — you were being lazy.
blocks.8.text: Not a single <div class="big-text">. Not a single paragraph with bars instead of a menu. Not a single heading that only looks like one. And not a single empty tag — a shell is not content.
blocks.10.text: When you are done, do this: close your eyes and read your code out loud, only the tags. Header. Nav. List of three links. Main. Heading one. Article. Heading two…
blocks.11.text: If that gives you a sensible picture of the page — you did it. If you hear group, group, group — go back and think again.
blocks.12.text: This is not an exercise. It is exactly what someone who cannot see your screen hears.
blocks.14.text: Do not start from the code. Draw the page on paper — rectangles. A strip at the top. Beside or below it — the menu. In the middle — the big part. At the bottom — a narrow strip.
blocks.15.text: Now name each rectangle. If you can name it with a word — this is the top part — there is a tag for it. The code is just writing the drawing down.
blocks.17.text: It will be ugly again. You know why by now, and you know it is not your fault.
blocks.18.text: But look at what is left under the ugliness: a page with meaning, that Google understands, a reader can speak, and a human can maintain. The looks are one file away. The meaning cannot be bolted on later.
